title: Using night-rate electricity to cut bills
subtitle: Time-of-use tariffs and how to shift flexible loads to cheaper overnight hours
excerpt: Time-of-use electricity tariffs charge less for electricity used overnight or at off-peak times. Households with flexible loads (dishwashers, washing machines, EV chargers, heat pump hot water, battery storage) can shift most of these loads to off-peak hours. The saving is 10 to 20p per kWh depending on the tariff, with an annual saving of 100 to 400 pounds in a typical household.
sourceNotes: Ofgem guidance on time-of-use tariffs (2024). Octopus Energy Go and Flux tariff structures. British Gas Electric Driver tariff. Uswitch analysis of time-of-use tariff uptake (2024). National Grid ESO flexibility documents.

glossaryTerms.definition: An electricity tariff where the unit rate varies by time of day. Overnight rates (typically 00:30 to 07:30 on Octopus Go) are cheaper, often 7 to 15p/kWh. Peak rates (usually 16:00 to 19:00) are more expensive, sometimes above 30p/kWh. Requires a smart meter.

content.text: is only useful if you can actually shift load. If your lifestyle means the dishwasher runs at 7 pm and the washing machine at 9 am, a flat-rate tariff may be cheaper. The first step is to work out what you can move and what you cannot.
Dishwasher: most modern dishwashers have a delay-start option. Set it before bed; it runs in the cheap window overnight.
Washing machine: as above. Wash at night. Drying in a tumble dryer also benefits from off-peak, though a heated airer or outdoor drying removes the dryer cost entirely.

EV charger: an EV with a smart charger can be set to begin charging at 00:30 and stop by 07:30. At 7p/kWh overnight vs 28p/kWh peak, the saving per charge is significant. A 40 kWh charge overnight costs 2.80 pounds instead of 11.20 pounds.

Heat pump hot water: schedule the DHW cycle to run between 01:00 and 06:00. This is also when outdoor temperatures are lowest, which reduces the heat pump's COP slightly, but the tariff saving usually outweighs the efficiency loss.
Home battery: charge the battery overnight at 7p/kWh and use stored power during peak periods at 28p. The saving per cycle is about 21p per kWh stored.
Space heating during the morning and evening: people are in the house and need heat at peak times. Time-of-use tariffs are less useful for households that heat mainly in the peak window.
Cooking: meal times are what they are.

A household with an EV, heat pump, and dishwasher: EV charges 25 kWh per week (saving 5.25 pounds/week vs peak), hot water 5 kWh per day shifted to overnight (saving 0.63 pounds/day × 180 days = 113 pounds), dishwasher 1 kWh per day shifted (saving 0.21 pounds/day × 300 days = 63 pounds). Total annual saving: roughly 400 pounds. The key requirement is a SMETS2 smart meter and a tariff that offers the right overnight window.

Octopus Go (7p overnight, 28p peak at time of writing), Octopus Cosy (cheaper mid-afternoon and overnight), and British Gas Electric Driver (cheap overnight for EV users) are common UK options. Check uswitch.com or the supplier's own site for current rates. Switch tariffs annually: prices change. The comparison sites allow you to upload half-hourly data from your smart meter to find the cheapest tariff for your specific usage pattern.
